turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next number oh one five ninety-five, the United States against Ruiz. General Olson...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Me- Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The Ninth Circuit has created a new constitutional rule for guilty pleas that is neither required by the Constitution nor warranted by this Court's previous decisions. Its inevitable effect would be to complicate and expose to collateral attack confessions of guilt which approximat- which account for approximately ninety-five percent of all convictions in the Federal system and to stifle the market for plea bargains, which this Court has described as an essential component of the administration of justice. The Ninth Circuit held that an accused cannot enter a valid guilty plea unless he is first given all evidence in the prosecutor's possession which would have a reasonable probability of discouraging him from pleading guilty. The Ninth Circuit's rule, new rule, is not a logical extension of the Brady Brady versus Maryland, which is premised on concern over the constitutional fairness of criminal trials. Brady and its progeny require f- disclosure only when necessary to ensure a fair trial. In fact, in Brady itself, the Court was explicit to point out that it that decision was premised on the avoidance of an unfair trial to the accused. The subsequent cases, which have expanded upon or interpreted or explained Brady, have been even more specific with respect to the limitations on the scope of Brady. In US versus Agurs, the Court said the prosecutor will not have violated his constitutional duty unless his omission is of sufficient significance to result in the denial of a fair trial. Something similar was said in US versus Bagley. Brady's purpose is not to displace the adversary system as the primary means by which truth is uncovered. If it did not deprive a defendant of a fair trial,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: there is no... i-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: constitutional violation.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can we get to your main argument about Brady, that is Brady in all its aspects is a trial right, not a pretrial right, in view of the plea agreement in this case, which represents that you have already turned over the prime Br- Brady material and the only question is the impeaching material?

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, I I'd respectfully disagree.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in the various...

turns.text: Well, th- th- th- there's some pretrial discovery right, but it's not extensive and it oftentimes it doesn't cover the types of information that has led to potential miscarriages of justice, a- as I set out in the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: brief.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: fact, the the the relevant discovery rule actually prohibits, as I read it, s- discovery of some material that you say this rule would cover.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: your...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: which is a troubling concept because one of the things we're we're sort of trying to do here is balance the system wide benefit of an a fast track program, on the one hand, with the occasional case where there's a risk of injustice that that concerns you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: doing is is you're asking us to open up the plea bargaining process and piecemeal to bring in a a constitutional rule that would affect one aspect of it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Can I...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: get a grasp of that by reading something?

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Do- d- d- d- under the fast track program, does the defendant have to waive rule sixteen rights?

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: The under the fast track program, the defendant can't file any motions at all, but the the what happens is is that there is a a pre indictment offer that's made and the pre indictment offer is usually accompanied by discovery in the form of i- in a case like Ms. Ruiz's, the reports of the initial inspectors and then the special agent who comes in and does the interrogation and does the sort of a summary of the other individuals'

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Now, those are available...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: even under the fast track program.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Suppose you're right on your constitutional argument.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, I I I agree, Justice Breyer, that there's no rule that you can say that a district court is compelled to depart in any case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: agreeing...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: really not what he said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: because you didn't sign it, right?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not proper.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: The the the district court's comment related to whether or not there he said to counsel that there was offer and acceptance and and that's it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What's bothering me...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: is this, that y- y- you could say, okay, let's just hold everything in abeyance, get to the issue.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, the Solicitor General hasn't been framing the questions related solely to the discovery issues, the Brady issue and the waiver issue.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Your your...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: answer...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: is an easy one, Mr. Hubachek.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: That's that's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: f- what I was I was...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: discuss the jurisdictional point, our our our decision would stand for nothing.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, perhaps cirt was was improvidently granted.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Did you argue in the Ninth Circuit that there was jurisdiction?

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Then I take it you certainly don't take a different position here.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: No, certainly not, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But our remedy would not be to...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: dismiss the writ.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You don't want that.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: No, I don't.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But but, you know, to say we'll just call it impermissible conduct because we want to get it done isn't very satisfactory.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But in order to make that argument, as I understand it, you have to make an unreliability argument divorced from a materiality argument.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: No.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: How do we judge that materiality at I mean, i- to in in Hill and Lockhart, when when you're dealing with counsel, you can at least say, well, if if they had been aware regardless of how the case would have turned out, there's a way in which it makes sense to say that if they had been aware of this kind of evidence, they would've said we're going to trial.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Or you've got to dispense with a materiality standard entirely and say anything that would've had any tendency to exculpate or to impeach in a way favorable to the defendant, if denied, supports in effect a a claim for relief, which is a non materiality standard.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, Justice Souter, on page sixteen of our brief, we have a block quote from Hill versus Lockhart.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: analysis...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: a different I mean, it necessarily is a different standard from the Brady standard of materiality which we have now.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, the Brady standard for materiality, as was explained in Kyles, derives from Strickland.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: it's...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: back to my question.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: Well, I I think that it's an easier application post trial, but it's still the same test that that's that we're being asked to apply in the plea situation because Hill versus Lockhart says, look, if counsel doesn't find the key piece of evidence and you plead guilty, then we're going to go back and look and see, well, what would've happened at a trial if you had that p- key piece of evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: you would prevail at trial...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: order to do that intelligently, we've got to know what the trial would've included, won't we? And that either means, number one, that the disclosure has got to go to, in effect, the inculpatory evidence, or it means at the minimum, number two, that the State has an opportunity to come in and say, we'll tell you what the inculpatory evidence would've been.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: W- and that's the same approach that Hill versus Lockhart requires.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the determination...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Hill it's easier because we know that trial decisions are are often made without knowing what the result would be.

turns.name: STEVEN F. HUBACHEK
turns.text: But Hill is a plea case.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Hubachek.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Olson, would you address again the jurisdictional problem here? I mean, if if in fact the district court judge had discretion about what sentence to impose and could've and did exercise that discretion, do we have to be concerned about juris-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think that is not an easy ca- situation, but I think that the Ninth Circuit believed that however inartfully the district court expressed it or incompletely the district court expressed it, that the that the district court was saying it didn't feel that it had the capacity or the ability under the law to depart, that it didn't have the discretion to do so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: otherwise to the Ninth Circuit...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: proper case could mean that, I suppose.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: could...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It could mean that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: perceived it.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: not to say anything about it, so long as I was not certain that there was no jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: We we believe that we after looking at it carefully, we've decided that the Ninth Circuit probably was right under the circumstances, although you could argue it the other way, and that this k- this is an issue that is presented and clearly with respect to the the the legal standard that's been adopted to the N- by the Ninth Circuit and which is in play today.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: well.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: you, General Olson.

turns.name: THE MARSHAL
turns.text: The honorable court is now ajourned until Monday next at ten o'clock...
